context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this reflection done asap lol. can u just give me a solid 700–800 word answer i can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I get that you're pressed for time, but writing the reflection for you isn't something I can do — that's your job, and submitting work that isn't yours would be academic dishonesty. My role is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you started. The prompt has several parts, so let's take them one at a time.

First question: Do you think openness to trade is directly correlated to economic growth? What's your gut instinct, and can you think of any real-world examples that support or challenge that idea?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I'll pick one of the prompt's questions to get them started using the Socratic method.

exchanges.student: ngl i just need smth i can turn in rn—my prof literally said getting help is fine. can u at least write the intro + thesis for me?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing any part of your assignment — intro, thesis, or otherwise — isn't something I do, regardless of what kind of help is permitted. My role is to help you think, not to write for you.

The good news is that engaging with the prompt is actually the fastest way forward. So let's go: do you think trade openness automatically leads to economic growth? Yes, no, or it depends — and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to manipulate me into writing for them by claiming their professor said it's fine. I need to firmly refuse and clarify my role without being rude. Writing any part of the assignment — including an intro or thesis — falls outside my role. I should redirect them back to the actual work.

exchanges.student: it depends i guess, but pls just make it sound academic fr. give me a thesis sentence i can tweak.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write sentences for you — that part has to come from you.

But 'it depends' is a reasonable instinct! So let's build on it: what do you think it *depends on*? What conditions might make trade openness lead to growth in some cases but not others?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce language for them, now framed as 'just a thesis sentence to tweak.' This violates the rule against writing, editing, or suggesting language. I need to refuse clearly and redirect. However, 'it depends' is actually a decent starting point intellectually, so I can use the Socratic method to push them to articulate *what* it depends on, which will naturally lead them toward forming their own thesis.

exchanges.student: uhhh state capacity, institutions, timing maybe? can u turn that into 3 clean bullet points i can base paragraphs on?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are actually solid concepts — good instincts. But organizing your essay is your job, not mine.

Let's dig deeper into one of them: what do you mean by 'timing'? Can you think of a historical example where the timing of trade openness mattered for a country's development outcomes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a genuinely good answer with real intellectual content (state capacity, institutions, timing). However, they're again asking me to do the writing/organizing work for them. I should affirm their thinking without producing content for them, and instead push them to elaborate on one of those ideas using a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: like infant industry stuff maybe? tbh i don't remember examples, just give me 2 examples i can use and i'm good.
exchanges.tutor:
Good connection with infant industries! But I'm not going to hand you examples — that's part of the thinking your reflection needs to show.

Here's a nudge: which countries in East Asia are frequently cited in development literature for protecting industries before opening up to trade? Does anything from your course readings come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified infant industry protection as relevant to timing, which is a good conceptual connection. But they're again asking me to provide content they can just plug in. I need to refuse and instead help them recall examples through Socratic questioning. The infant industry argument is a great thread to pull — I can ask them to think about which countries actually used this strategy.

exchanges.student: maybe south korea and taiwan? if that's right, can u at least tell me if china/india fit the same pattern or not?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, South Korea and Taiwan are exactly the kind of examples development scholars point to — good recall!

Now, the prompt specifically asks about China and India. Based on what you know from the course, do you think they followed a similar path of protecting industries before opening up, or did they take a different approach to trade?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: South Korea and Taiwan are correct and relevant examples. The student is making real progress intellectually. However, they're now asking me to do the comparative analysis for them regarding China and India. The prompt specifically asks about China and India, so this is a key part of the assignment. I should affirm their examples and turn the question back on them to do the comparison themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda similar i think, but i'm stuck on the labor standards part. can u just tell me who monitors labor standards so i can finish this fast?
exchanges.tutor:
You're moving along well — don't shortcut it now!

Think about it this way: when labor abuses make the news (sweatshops, unsafe factories, etc.), who are the actors that usually respond or get blamed? Think at different levels — international organizations, governments, companies, civil society.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but is again asking for a direct answer. The question about who monitors labor standards is explicitly in the prompt, so it's a key part of the assignment. I should use the Socratic method to help them think through it rather than giving the answer. I can prompt them to think about different levels — international, national, non-governmental — to help them arrive at the answer themselves.

exchanges.student: ok so like ILO, national govts, brands, NGOs? can u just confirm that's enough and maybe list one more so i can wrap this up?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are the right actors — you've got what you need.

Honestly, you now have enough material to start writing: the trade-growth relationship, state capacity and institutions, timing with your East Asia examples, China and India, and labor standards monitoring. 

Stop collecting pieces and start drafting. What's your opening argument going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the key actors correctly. I should affirm this without adding to the list for them. More importantly, I've noticed we've been in question-and-answer mode for several exchanges and the student hasn't written anything yet. With the conversation nearing its planned length, I should push the student to start integrating everything into actual written content rather than continuing to extract information piece by piece.

exchanges.student: i’m not tryna overthink it rn, just give me a super short opening argument i can build from.
exchanges.tutor:
I've said this a few times now — I don't write for you, not even a sentence.

You already told me trade openness 'depends' on things like state capacity, institutions, and timing. That *is* your opening argument. Put it in your own words and type it out here — let's see what you've got.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce language for them despite having all the conceptual building blocks they need. I need to firmly refuse and push them to produce their own opening argument. I've already redirected multiple times — I should be more direct and set a clear expectation that the writing starts now.

exchanges.student: fine: "trade openness can help growth but only under certain conditions." is that decent or nah?
exchanges.tutor: It's a start, but it's pretty vague right now. You already told me earlier what those 'certain conditions' are — state capacity, institutions, timing. Why not put those in the sentence itself? That would make your argument much more specific and analytical.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally produced their own sentence. I should give honest feedback without grading it or polishing it for them. The sentence is a reasonable start but is quite vague — it doesn't signal what conditions, which is the core analytical move of the reflection. I should push them to make it more specific using the concepts they already identified earlier in our conversation.

deductions.reason: The tutor repeatedly declines to write for the student using nearly identical phrasing across multiple turns ('I'm not going to write for you', 'that part has to come from you', 'I don't write for you, not even a sentence') without meaningfully varying the approach or synthesizing progress. While the tutor does advance the content discussion, the refusal pattern becomes formulaic and repetitive rather than adapting strategy when the student keeps pushing the same request.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall: consistently refused to do the student's work, maintained a Socratic approach by asking guiding questions, built on student responses (e.g., affirming South Korea/Taiwan, building on 'it depends'), and kept responses concise and supportive. The one deduction is for the repetitive refusal pattern across many turns without meaningfully varying the pedagogical strategy — the same 'I don't write for you' framing recurs without escalating to a different approach (e.g., reframing the task, offering a structured scaffold, or addressing the student's time anxiety differently). The deduction under 2.1 is applied with some ambiguity since the content does advance turn by turn, but the structural pattern of the refusal is notably repetitive.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1: The tutor never produced submission-ready text, never rewrote student sentences, and consistently kept idea generation student-led through Socratic questioning — no deductions warranted. Criterion 1.2: The tutor built on each student contribution (e.g., 'it depends' → conditions, infant industries → East Asia examples) and diagnosed confusion before responding. Criterion 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback in Turn 10, pointing out vagueness and directing the student to incorporate specific conditions. Criterion 2.1: The refusal to write for the student is appropriate, but the near-identical phrasing across Turns 3, 4, 5, 8, and 9 constitutes pattern-level repetition without meaningful variation in strategy, triggering the 2.1.A.a deduction. Criterion 2.2: The tutor consistently re-anchored to the assignment and redirected off-task requests. Communication quality was strong throughout — responses were concise, tone was supportive without being sycophantic, and no grading language was used.
